hints: Powers of two have exactly one bit set in their binary representation.
Subtracting 1 from a power of two flips the set bit and sets all lower bits.
n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit — for powers of two this yields 0.
approach: A number n is a power of two if and only if n > 0 and n & (n-1) == 0. The expression n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit; for powers of two (which have exactly one set bit), this always produces 0.
time_complexity_explanation: A single bitwise AND operation and comparison — constant time regardless of input.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space is used.
key_insight: Powers of two have exactly one set bit, so n & (n-1) always removes that lone bit, giving 0 — an elegant O(1) bitwise check.

title: Longest Turbulent Subarray
hints: A turbulent subarray alternates strictly between increasing and decreasing comparisons.
Use a sliding window: expand right and shrink left when the turbulence condition breaks.
The window breaks when two consecutive comparisons have the same sign or a comparison is equal.
approach: Use a sliding window with a left pointer. For each right >= 1, compute c = sign(arr[right] - arr[right-1]). If right >= 2, compare with the previous comparison sign: if c == 0 reset left to right; if c equals the previous sign, the last valid window starts at right-1. Update result each iteration.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with O(1) work per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointer and comparison variables are used.
key_insight: When the turbulence breaks due to two consecutive same-direction comparisons, the new window must start from the previous element (right-1), not right — because (right-1, right) still forms a valid 2-element turbulent window.

title: Maximum Points You Can Obtain from Cards
hints: You take k cards from either end — equivalently, you leave n-k consecutive cards in the middle.
Instead of maximizing the cards you take, minimize the sum of the n-k cards you leave.
Slide a window of size n-k across the array to find the minimum subarray sum.
approach: Reframe: picking k cards from either end leaves a contiguous subarray of size n-k in the middle. Use a sliding window of fixed size n-k to find the minimum subarray sum. The answer is the total sum minus this minimum window sum.
time_complexity_explanation: One pass to compute total, one pass for the sliding window — both O(n) where n is the number of cards.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of sum variables are maintained.
key_insight: Picking k cards from either end is equivalent to NOT picking a contiguous window of n-k cards — minimizing the middle window maximizes the score.

title: Factorial Trailing Zeroes
hints: Trailing zeroes come from factors of 10 = 2 * 5; there are always more factors of 2 than 5 in n!.
Count how many multiples of 5 are in n!, then multiples of 25, 125, etc.
Sum floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + floor(n/125) + ... until the term is zero.
approach: Trailing zeroes in n! are produced by factors of 10, each requiring one factor of 2 and one factor of 5. Since factors of 2 are always more abundant, the count of trailing zeroes equals the count of factors of 5 in n!. This is computed by summing floor(n/5^k) for k=1,2,3,... until 5^k > n.
time_complexity_explanation: The loop runs log_5(n) times since power multiplies by 5 each iteration.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The number of times 5 divides n! is floor(n/5) + floor(n/25) + ... because every multiple of 25 contributes an extra factor of 5 beyond what floor(n/5) counts, and similarly for higher powers.

title: Second Highest Salary
hints: Use LIMIT and OFFSET to skip the highest salary and grab the next one.
Wrap the query in a subquery or use IFNULL/COALESCE to return NULL when no second salary exists.
Use DISTINCT to avoid counting duplicate top salaries as distinct ranks.
approach: Select the maximum salary that is strictly less than the overall maximum, or equivalently use LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1 on a DESC-ordered DISTINCT salary list. Wrapping in IFNULL/COALESCE ensures NULL is returned when fewer than two distinct salaries exist.
solution_python:
-- SQL Solution
SELECT IFNULL(
    (SELECT DISTINCT salary
     FROM Employee
     ORDER BY salary DESC
     LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1),
    NULL
) AS SecondHighestSalary;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Sorting all employee salaries to find the second distinct maximum takes O(n log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant amount of extra space is needed beyond the input table.
key_insight: Using DISTINCT before ordering ensures duplicate top salaries don't masquerade as the second-highest; IFNULL handles the edge case of fewer than two distinct values.

title: Duplicate Emails
hints: Group rows by the email column and count occurrences.
Filter groups where the count is greater than 1.
Use HAVING to filter after GROUP BY.
approach: Group the Person table by email and count occurrences. Use HAVING COUNT(*) > 1 to retain only emails that appear more than once. SELECT that email column as the result.
solution_python:
SELECT email AS Email
FROM Person
GROUP BY email
HAVING COUNT(*) > 1;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The database scans all n rows to build the group aggregates.
space_complexity_explanation: Intermediate grouping may require O(n) space for the hash table of email counts.
key_insight: GROUP BY + HAVING is the idiomatic SQL pattern for finding duplicates; the HAVING clause filters aggregated groups, unlike WHERE which filters individual rows.

title: Reverse String
hints: Use two pointers — one at the start, one at the end — and swap characters as they move inward.
You must do this in-place with O(1) extra memory.
The loop ends when the two pointers meet in the middle.
approach: Initialize two pointers at the beginning and end of the array. Swap the characters at both pointers, then move them inward. Repeat until they meet.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is visited at most once as the two pointers sweep inward.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointer variables are used — the reversal is done in-place.
key_insight: Two-pointer swap from both ends is the canonical O(1) space in-place reversal.

title: String to Integer (atoi)
hints: Handle leading whitespace first, then an optional '+' or '-' sign.
Read digits one by one and build the number. Stop at the first non-digit character.
Clamp the result to the 32-bit signed integer range [-2^31, 2^31 - 1].
approach: Skip whitespace, read optional sign, then accumulate digits. Before adding each digit, check for overflow by comparing with INT_MAX/10. Clamp to 32-bit range.
time_complexity_explanation: We scan the string once from left to right.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Methodically handle each parsing stage (whitespace → sign → digits → clamp) to avoid missed edge cases.

title: Reverse Words in a String
hints: Split the string by spaces, filter out empty strings, then reverse the list of words.
For an in-place approach: reverse the entire string, then reverse each individual word.
Watch out for leading, trailing, and multiple consecutive spaces.
approach: Split the string into words (ignoring extra spaces), reverse the list, and join with single spaces. For O(1) extra space in languages with mutable strings, reverse the entire string first, then reverse each word in place.
time_complexity_explanation: Splitting and reversing both take linear time in the length of the string.
space_complexity_explanation: The split creates a list of words, and the result string uses O(n) space.
key_insight: Splitting by whitespace naturally handles multiple spaces; reversing the word array gives the answer.

title: Number of 1 Bits
hints: Repeatedly check whether the lowest bit of n is 1, then shift n right by 1.
A cleaner trick: n & (n-1) clears the lowest set bit; count how many times you can do this before n becomes 0.
Python's built-in bin() converts n to a binary string; counting '1' characters gives the answer in one line.
approach: Use the bit trick n & (n-1) which clears the lowest set bit of n. Count how many times this operation can be applied before n reaches 0 — that count equals the number of 1 bits (Hamming weight).
time_complexity_explanation: The loop iterates exactly k times where k is the number of set bits, which is at most 32 for a 32-bit integer.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single counter variable is used regardless of input size.
key_insight: The expression n & (n-1) always clears exactly the lowest set bit, so looping until n is zero counts set bits with minimal iterations.

title: Excel Sheet Column Number
hints: Think of column titles as base-26 numbers where A=1, B=2, ..., Z=26.
Process characters left to right: multiply the running total by 26, then add the current character's value.
The value of a character is ord(c) - ord('A') + 1.
approach: Treat the column title as a base-26 number. Iterate left to right, multiplying the accumulator by 26 and adding the value of the current letter (A=1 through Z=26).
time_complexity_explanation: We process each character of the column title exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a single accumulator variable is used.
key_insight: Excel columns are essentially base-26 with A=1 instead of 0, so standard positional notation applies.

title: First Unique Character in a String
hints: Count the frequency of each character using a hash map or array of size 26.
Make a second pass to find the first character with a count of 1.
Return the index of that character, or -1 if no unique character exists.
approach: First pass: count frequency of every character using a hash map. Second pass: return the index of the first character whose count is exactly 1.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes through the string.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most 26 lowercase English letters — constant space.
key_insight: Two-pass counting: first count frequencies, then scan for the first character with count 1.

title: Add Binary
hints: Process both strings from the rightmost digit, just like manual addition.
Keep a carry variable. At each position: sum = digit_a + digit_b + carry.
The current bit is sum % 2, and the new carry is sum // 2.
approach: Traverse both strings from right to left, adding corresponding bits along with a carry. Append sum % 2 to the result and update carry to sum // 2. After both strings are exhausted, add any remaining carry. Reverse the result.
time_complexity_explanation: We process each digit of both strings once, taking time proportional to the longer string.
space_complexity_explanation: The result string has at most max(m, n) + 1 characters.
key_insight: Binary addition follows the same carry-propagation logic as decimal addition — process right to left with a carry bit.

title: Path Sum
hints: Subtract the current node's value from targetSum as you recurse down.
A leaf node is one with no left or right child.
At a leaf, check if the remaining targetSum equals the leaf's value.
approach: DFS from root, subtracting each node's value from targetSum. At a leaf node, check if the remaining target equals the node's value. Return True if any root-to-leaf path sums to targetSum.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit each node once in the worst case.
space_complexity_explanation: The recursion stack depth is the height h of the tree — O(log n) for balanced, O(n) for skewed.
key_insight: Reduce targetSum as you descend; at a leaf, check if the remaining sum equals the leaf value.

title: Path Sum II
hints: This extends Path Sum — now you need to collect all valid root-to-leaf paths.
Use backtracking: add the current node to the path, recurse, then remove it when returning.
At a leaf, if the remaining sum equals the leaf value, copy the current path to the result.
approach: DFS with backtracking. Maintain a running path list. At each node, append its value and subtract from the target. At a leaf where the target is met, add a copy of the path to results. Pop the node when backtracking.
time_complexity_explanation: We visit all n nodes. In the worst case (balanced tree), we copy O(log n) paths of length O(log n), but for a skewed tree with many valid paths, copying dominates at O(n²).
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack is O(h), and the current path is O(h). Output storage is separate.
key_insight: Backtracking with path.append/path.pop lets you reuse one list to explore all root-to-leaf paths efficiently.

title: Minimum Size Subarray Sum
hints: Use a sliding window (two pointers) that expands to the right and contracts from the left.
Expand the window until the sum is >= target, then shrink from the left to find the minimum length.
Track the minimum window length across all valid windows.
approach: Maintain a sliding window with left and right pointers. Expand right to grow the sum. Once sum >= target, shrink from the left while maintaining the condition, updating the minimum length at each step.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is added and removed from the window at most once, so total operations are O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few pointer and sum variables are used.
key_insight: Shrink the window from the left whenever the condition is met — this guarantees finding the minimum-length valid subarray.

title: Find All Anagrams in a String
hints: Use a fixed-size sliding window of length len(p) over string s.
Maintain a frequency count of the current window and compare it to p's frequency count.
Optimize: track how many characters have matching frequencies to avoid comparing full maps each time.
approach: Slide a window of size len(p) across s. Maintain character frequency counts for the window. When the window's frequency matches p's frequency, record the start index. Update counts incrementally as the window slides.
time_complexity_explanation: We slide the window across s once. Array comparison is O(26) = O(1) at each step.
space_complexity_explanation: Two fixed-size arrays of 26 characters — constant space.
key_insight: A fixed-size sliding window with frequency counts turns anagram detection into an incremental comparison problem.

title: Swap Nodes in Pairs
hints: Use a dummy node before the head to simplify edge cases.
For each pair, you need to adjust three pointers: prev.next, first.next, and second.next.
After swapping, advance prev by two nodes to the next pair.
approach: Use a dummy node. For each pair of adjacent nodes, rewire pointers to swap them: prev → second → first → rest. Advance the pointer by two steps and repeat.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def swapPairs(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> Optional[ListNode]:
        dummy = ListNode(0, head)
        prev = dummy
        while prev.next and prev.next.next:
            first = prev.next
            second = first.next
            first.next = second.next
            second.next = first
            prev.next = second
            prev = first
        return dummy.next
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse the list once, processing two nodes at a time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointer variables are used.
key_insight: A dummy head node eliminates special-casing for the first pair; then it's a repetitive 3-pointer rewiring.

title: Odd Even Linked List
hints: Separate the list into two chains: odd-indexed nodes and even-indexed nodes.
Use two pointers — odd and even — and alternate linking nodes to each chain.
After traversal, attach the even chain to the end of the odd chain.
approach: Maintain two pointers for odd and even indexed nodes. Iterate through the list, linking each odd node to the next odd and each even node to the next even. Finally, connect the tail of the odd list to the head of the even list.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def oddEvenList(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> Optional[ListNode]:
        if not head:
            return head
        odd = head
        even = head.next
        even_head = even
        while even and even.next:
            odd.next = even.next
            odd = odd.next
            even.next = odd.next
            even = even.next
        odd.next = even_head
        return head
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse each node exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few pointer variables are used — rearrangement is in-place.
key_insight: Splitting into odd/even chains in one pass and then reconnecting avoids complex index tracking.

title: Sort List
hints: Merge sort is ideal for linked lists — splitting at the midpoint and merging are both natural operations.
Use slow/fast pointers to find the middle of the list.
Recursively sort each half, then merge the two sorted halves.
approach: Apply merge sort on the linked list. Find the midpoint using slow/fast pointers, split the list in two, recursively sort each half, then merge the two sorted halves using a standard merge procedure.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def sortList(self, head: Optional[ListNode]) -> Optional[ListNode]:
        if not head or not head.next:
            return head
        slow, fast = head, head.next
        while fast and fast.next:
            slow = slow.next
            fast = fast.next.next
        mid = slow.next
        slow.next = None
        left = self.sortList(head)
        right = self.sortList(mid)
        dummy = ListNode(0)
        curr = dummy
        while left and right:
            if left.val <= right.val:
                curr.next = left
                left = left.next
            else:
                curr.next = right
                right = right.next
            curr = curr.next
        curr.next = left or right
        return dummy.next
time_complexity_explanation: Merge sort divides the list in half (log n levels) and merges n elements at each level.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion depth is O(log n). No extra arrays are needed — merging is done in-place by pointer manipulation.
key_insight: Merge sort on linked lists is naturally O(1) extra space (excluding recursion stack) since merging doesn't require auxiliary arrays.

title: Reverse Linked List II
hints: Navigate to the node just before position 'left' — this is your anchor.
Reverse the sublist from 'left' to 'right' using standard reversal.
Reconnect the reversed segment to the rest of the list.
approach: Use a dummy node. Advance to the node before 'left'. Then reverse the sublist of (right - left + 1) nodes in place using repeated insertion at the front of the reversed section. Reconnect the reversed portion.
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse the list once to reach 'left', then reverse (right - left) links.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of pointer variables are used.
key_insight: Repeatedly move the node after 'curr' to the front of the reversed section — this reverses in one pass without extra space.

title: Count Primes
hints: A naive approach checks each number for primality, but that's too slow for large n.
The Sieve of Eratosthenes efficiently marks all composites: start from 2 and mark every multiple.
You only need to sieve up to sqrt(n) since any composite number has a factor no larger than its square root.
approach: Apply the Sieve of Eratosthenes: create a boolean array of size n initialized to True. Starting from 2, for each prime p found, mark all multiples of p (starting from p*p) as composite. Count all indices still marked True at the end (indices 2 through n-1).
time_complexity_explanation: The Sieve of Eratosthenes runs in O(n log log n) time due to the harmonic series of prime reciprocals.
space_complexity_explanation: A boolean array of size n is allocated to track which numbers are prime.
key_insight: Starting the inner loop at p*p (rather than 2*p) is the key optimization: all smaller multiples of p have already been marked by smaller primes.

title: Maximum Width of Binary Tree
hints: Assign each node an index — for node at index i, left child is 2i and right child is 2i+1.
The width of a level is (last_index - first_index + 1) including null positions.
Use BFS and track the index of each node at every level.
approach: BFS with index tracking. Assign the root index 0. For each level, record the first and last indices. Width = last - first + 1. The maximum across all levels is the answer. Normalize indices per level to prevent overflow.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited exactly once during BFS.
space_complexity_explanation: The queue can hold up to n/2 nodes at the widest level.
key_insight: Treat the tree as a complete binary tree using index numbering — width is just the difference between the leftmost and rightmost index on each level.

title: All Nodes Distance K in Binary Tree
hints: A tree node can only reach its children directly. To go 'upward', you need parent pointers.
First, build a parent map using DFS. Then BFS from the target node to distance K.
Use a visited set to avoid revisiting nodes during BFS.
approach: First pass: DFS to build a map from each node to its parent. Second pass: BFS from the target node, expanding to left child, right child, and parent. Stop at distance K and collect all nodes at that level.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the parent map visits all n nodes. BFS also visits each node at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The parent map, visited set, and BFS queue each use O(n) space.
key_insight: Convert the tree into an undirected graph by adding parent pointers, then BFS from target to exactly distance K.

title: Accounts Merge
hints: Two accounts belong to the same person if they share at least one email.
Model emails as nodes in a graph. Emails within the same account are connected.
Use Union-Find (or DFS) to group connected emails, then associate each group with the account name.
approach: Use Union-Find. For each account, union all its emails together. Then group emails by their root parent. For each group, sort the emails and prepend the account name.
time_complexity_explanation: n accounts with average k emails each. Union-Find operations are nearly O(1) amortized (inverse Ackermann). Sorting emails adds O(n·k·log(n·k)).
space_complexity_explanation: The parent map and email-to-name map each store O(n·k) entries.
key_insight: Union-Find naturally merges accounts that share any email — union all emails within each account, then group by root.

title: Reverse Integer
hints: Extract digits from the end using x % 10 and x // 10.
Build the reversed number by: result = result * 10 + digit.
Check for 32-bit signed integer overflow before each multiplication.
approach: Repeatedly extract the last digit with modulo and build the reversed number. Before appending each digit, check if the result would overflow the 32-bit signed integer range [-2^31, 2^31 - 1]. Return 0 on overflow.
time_complexity_explanation: The number of digits in x is O(log10(x)), and we process each digit once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: The overflow check must happen before multiplying by 10 — compare result with INT_MAX/10 to catch it early.

title: Zigzag Conversion
hints: Create an array of strings, one per row. Simulate the zigzag by toggling direction.
Move down through rows 0 to numRows-1, then back up from numRows-2 to 1, repeatedly.
Concatenate all row strings at the end.
approach: Create numRows buckets. Iterate through the string, appending each character to the current row. Toggle direction when you hit row 0 or numRows-1. Join all rows for the result.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is visited once and appended to a row.
space_complexity_explanation: The row strings collectively store all n characters.
key_insight: Simulate the zigzag with a direction toggle — no need to compute index formulas.

title: Number of Provinces
hints: This is a connected components problem on an undirected graph.
Use DFS/BFS from each unvisited city, marking all reachable cities as visited.
Alternatively, Union-Find: union connected cities and count distinct roots.
approach: DFS/BFS to count connected components. For each unvisited city, start a DFS to visit all directly and indirectly connected cities. Each DFS launch increments the province count.
time_complexity_explanation: For each city, we potentially check all n connections. With n cities, total work is O(n²).
space_complexity_explanation: Visited set and recursion stack each use O(n) space.
key_insight: Province count = number of connected components. Each DFS from an unvisited node discovers one full component.

title: Shortest Path in Binary Matrix
hints: BFS from (0,0) guarantees finding the shortest path in an unweighted grid.
You can move in 8 directions (including diagonals).
If the start or end cell is blocked (value 1), return -1 immediately.
approach: BFS from top-left corner. Each cell has 8 neighbors. Track visited cells to avoid revisiting. The first time we reach the bottom-right corner, the BFS depth + 1 is the shortest path length.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the n² cells is visited at most once. Each has up to 8 neighbors to check.
space_complexity_explanation: The BFS queue can hold up to O(n²) cells in the worst case.
key_insight: BFS on an unweighted grid naturally finds the shortest path — the first arrival at any cell is optimal.

title: Longest Palindrome
hints: A palindrome uses characters in pairs (mirrored). Count character frequencies.
Every character with an even count fully contributes. Odd-count characters contribute count - 1.
You can place one odd-count character in the center, so add 1 if any odd counts exist.
approach: Count character frequencies. Sum up even parts of each count. If any character has an odd count, we can place one in the middle, so add 1 to the total.
time_complexity_explanation: One pass to count characters, one pass over the (at most 52) frequency entries.
space_complexity_explanation: At most 52 distinct characters (a-z, A-Z) — constant space.
key_insight: Palindromes are built from pairs of characters plus at most one center character — count pairs greedily.

title: Binary Tree Inorder Traversal
hints: Recursive: go left, visit node, go right.
Iterative: use a stack. Push all left children, then pop and visit, then move to the right child.
Morris traversal achieves O(1) space by threading the tree.
approach: Iterative approach using a stack. Push nodes going left until null. Pop the top node, add its value to the result, then move to its right child and repeat.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is pushed and popped from the stack exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack holds up to O(h) nodes and the result stores all n values.
key_insight: The iterative inorder pattern: go as far left as possible, process, then step right — memorize this template.

title: Construct BST from Preorder Traversal
hints: In preorder, the first element is the root. All smaller values go left, larger go right.
Use an upper bound to decide when to stop building the left subtree.
Pass bounds recursively: each node's value must be less than its upper bound.
approach: Use a recursive builder with an upper bound. The first unused value becomes the root if it's within bounds. Recurse for the left subtree (bound = root.val) then right subtree (bound = parent's bound). Use an index pointer to track position in preorder.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is processed exactly once — the index only moves forward.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth is O(n) in the worst case (skewed tree).
key_insight: Upper bound recursion: each call consumes elements that fit within the current bound, naturally partitioning left and right subtrees.

title: Random Pick with Weight
hints: Build a prefix sum array from the weights.
Generate a random number in [1, total_weight].
Binary search the prefix sum array to find which index that random number falls into.
approach: Precompute a prefix sum of weights. To pick, generate a random integer in [1, total], then binary search the prefix sum for the leftmost index where prefix[i] >= random. This index is the weighted random pick.
time_complexity_explanation: Building the prefix sum is O(n). Each pickIndex call binary searches in O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: The prefix sum array stores n values.
key_insight: Prefix sums create contiguous ranges proportional to weights — binary search maps a random number to the correct index in O(log n).

title: Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days
hints: Binary search on the ship capacity. The minimum is max(weights), the maximum is sum(weights).
For a given capacity, greedily check if all packages can be shipped within D days.
If you can ship in ≤ D days, try a smaller capacity; otherwise try larger.
approach: Binary search on the answer (ship capacity). For each candidate capacity, simulate greedy loading: add packages to the current day until the capacity would be exceeded, then start a new day. If days needed ≤ D, the capacity works.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search over S = sum(weights) - max(weights) range. Each check scans n weights. Total: O(n log S).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few variables are used for the binary search and simulation.
key_insight: When the answer is monotonic (larger capacity → fewer days), binary search on the answer space is the go-to technique.

title: Find First and Last Position of Element in Sorted Array
hints: Use two binary searches: one to find the leftmost (first) occurrence, one for the rightmost (last).
For leftmost: when you find target, keep searching left (hi = mid).
For rightmost: when you find target, keep searching right (lo = mid + 1), then return lo - 1.
approach: Run binary search twice. First search finds the leftmost index where nums[i] >= target. Second search finds the leftmost index where nums[i] > target, then subtract 1. If the found positions are valid and contain the target, return them.
time_complexity_explanation: Two binary searches, each O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few pointer variables are used.
key_insight: bisect_left(target+1) - 1 gives the rightmost occurrence — this avoids writing a separate 'find last' binary search.

title: Missing Number
hints: The array contains n distinct numbers from 0 to n. One number is missing.
Math approach: expected sum = n*(n+1)/2. Subtract the actual sum.
XOR approach: XOR all indices 0..n with all array values — the missing number remains.
approach: XOR all numbers from 0 to n and XOR all elements in the array. Since x ^ x = 0, all paired numbers cancel out, leaving only the missing number.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array.
space_complexity_explanation: Only one variable used for the XOR accumulator.
key_insight: XOR is its own inverse — pairing each index with each value cancels everything except the missing number.

title: Single Number
hints: Every element appears twice except one. Think about a bitwise operation where x ^ x = 0.
XOR all elements together — duplicate pairs cancel out.
The result is the single element that has no pair.
approach: XOR all elements in the array. Since a ^ a = 0 and a ^ 0 = a, all duplicate pairs cancel, leaving only the unique element.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with one XOR per element.
space_complexity_explanation: Only one accumulator variable is used.
key_insight: XOR cancels paired duplicates (x ^ x = 0) and preserves the unique element (x ^ 0 = x).

title: Maximal Square
hints: Define dp[i][j] as the side length of the largest square with bottom-right corner at (i, j).
If matrix[i][j] == '1': dp[i][j] = min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1], dp[i-1][j-1]) + 1.
The answer is the square of the maximum dp value.
approach: DP where dp[i][j] = side length of the largest all-ones square ending at (i,j). Transition: min of three neighbors (top, left, top-left) + 1. Track the global maximum side length.
time_complexity_explanation: We fill each cell of the m × n DP table exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The DP table has (m+1) × (n+1) entries. Can be optimized to O(n) with a rolling row.
key_insight: A square of side k at (i,j) requires squares of side k-1 at all three adjacent corners — hence the min of three neighbors.

title: Game of Life
hints: The challenge is updating in-place — you need to know original states while computing new ones.
Encode both states in the cell: use 2 to mean 'was alive, now dead' and 3 for 'was dead, now alive'.
In a second pass, convert encoded values: cells with value 2 → 0, cells with value 3 → 1.
approach: Use intermediate states to encode transitions. 0→0 stays 0, 1→0 becomes 2, 0→1 becomes 3, 1→1 stays 1. Count live neighbors using original state (value % 2 for current, value >= 2 for transition). Apply rules, then decode.
time_complexity_explanation: Two passes over the m × n board. Each cell checks up to 8 neighbors — constant work per cell.
space_complexity_explanation: State transitions are encoded in-place using intermediate values — no extra board needed.
key_insight: Encode 'was alive' vs 'is alive' in the cell value itself (2 = was alive→dead, 3 = was dead→alive) to update in-place.

title: Summary Ranges
hints: Use a pointer to mark the start of each consecutive range, then extend it as long as consecutive elements differ by 1.
When the consecutive sequence breaks (or you reach the end), record the range as either 'a' (single element) or 'a->b' (multi-element).
Move the start pointer to the next element after the break and repeat.
approach: Iterate through the sorted unique array with a start pointer. For each position, advance an end pointer while consecutive elements differ by exactly 1. When the sequence breaks, record either 'start' if start equals end, or 'start->end' otherwise. Move start past the end and continue.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is visited at most twice (once by the outer loop and once by the inner advance), giving linear time.
space_complexity_explanation: Only pointer variables and the output list are used; the output is not counted as extra space.
key_insight: Tracking the start of each run and extending until a gap is found lets you emit each range in one step without any backtracking.

title: Design Linked List
hints: Use a dummy head (sentinel) node to simplify insertion and deletion at any position.
Track the size of the list to validate indices quickly.
For addAtIndex and deleteAtIndex, traverse to the node just before the target position.
approach: Use a singly linked list with a sentinel head node. Maintain a size counter. For get/add/delete at index, traverse from the sentinel to the node before the target. The sentinel eliminates special cases for head operations.
time_complexity_explanation: get, addAtIndex, and deleteAtIndex traverse up to n nodes. addAtHead is O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: The list stores n nodes plus one sentinel node.
key_insight: A sentinel head node unifies all insertion/deletion logic — you always operate on prev.next, never special-case the head.

title: Number of Digit One
hints: Count the contribution of digit '1' at each position (ones, tens, hundreds, etc.) separately.
For a digit position with factor f, compute: higher = n // (f * 10), current = (n // f) % 10, lower = n % f.
If current == 0: count += higher * f; if current == 1: count += higher * f + lower + 1; if current > 1: count += (higher + 1) * f.
approach: Iterate over each digit position (factor = 1, 10, 100, ...). For each position compute: higher digits (n // (factor*10)), current digit ((n // factor) % 10), and lower digits (n % factor). The number of 1s contributed by this position follows three cases based on whether the current digit is 0, 1, or greater than 1. Sum contributions across all positions.
time_complexity_explanation: The loop iterates once per digit of n, which is O(log n) iterations.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Counting digit '1' position by position and using the three-case formula (current < 1, == 1, > 1) avoids brute-force enumeration entirely, reducing complexity from O(n log n) to O(log n).

title: Open the Lock
hints: Each lock state is a node. Two states are connected if they differ by one digit ±1.
BFS from '0000' to the target gives the minimum turns.
Add all deadends to the visited set before starting BFS to avoid them.
approach: BFS from '0000'. Each state has 8 neighbors (4 wheels × 2 directions). Pre-add all deadends to the visited set. The first time we reach the target, the BFS depth is the answer.
time_complexity_explanation: There are at most 10,000 possible states (0000-9999). Each state has 8 neighbors to check.
space_complexity_explanation: The visited set and queue store up to 10,000 states.
key_insight: The lock state space is a graph with 10⁴ nodes — BFS naturally finds the shortest path while avoiding deadends.

title: Minimum Knight Moves
hints: BFS from (0,0) exploring all 8 knight moves. Use symmetry: work in the first quadrant (abs(x), abs(y)).
Limit the search space — the knight never needs to go far beyond the target.
Alternatively, use the mathematical formula for large distances.
approach: BFS from (0,0). Exploit symmetry by converting target to first quadrant (|x|, |y|). Explore all 8 knight moves, pruning positions that go too far negative (allow small negative to handle edge cases near origin). First reach of the target gives minimum moves.
time_complexity_explanation: BFS explores cells within a roughly circular area of radius proportional to the target distance.
space_complexity_explanation: Visited set and queue store all explored positions.
key_insight: Symmetry reduction (first quadrant) and pruning (don't go too negative) make BFS feasible for the infinite board.

title: Paint House
hints: dp[i][c] = minimum cost to paint houses 0..i where house i is color c.
Transition: dp[i][c] = costs[i][c] + min(dp[i-1][other colors]).
You only need the previous row — optimize space to O(1) with three variables.
approach: DP where dp[i][c] is the min cost to paint houses 0 through i with house i painted color c. For each house, try all 3 colors, adding the current cost to the minimum of the other two colors from the previous house.
time_complexity_explanation: One pass through n houses with constant work per house (3 colors).
space_complexity_explanation: Only three variables track the running costs — no DP table needed.
key_insight: With only 3 colors, the DP transition is simple: each color takes the min of the other two from the previous house.

title: Insert Delete GetRandom O(1)
hints: Use a list (array) for O(1) random access and a hash map for O(1) lookup.
For O(1) removal: swap the element to remove with the last element, then pop.
The hash map stores value → index for instant position lookup.
approach: Maintain an array of values and a hash map (value → index). Insert appends to the array and updates the map. Remove swaps the target with the last element, updates the swapped element's index in the map, then pops. getRandom picks a random array index.
time_complexity_explanation: Insert, remove, and getRandom are all O(1) average. The swap-to-end trick makes removal O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: The array and hash map each store n elements.
key_insight: Swap-to-last-and-pop makes array removal O(1) — combined with a hash map for O(1) lookup, all three operations are constant time.

title: My Calendar I
hints: Two events [s1, e1) and [s2, e2) overlap if s1 < e2 AND s2 < e1.
Maintain a sorted list of booked intervals.
For each new booking, binary search to find where it would go and check neighbors for overlap.
approach: Keep a sorted list of (start, end) intervals. For each new booking, use binary search to find the insertion point. Check the neighboring intervals for overlap. If no overlap, insert the new interval.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search is O(log n), but insertion into a sorted array is O(n) due to shifting. Overall O(n) per call.
space_complexity_explanation: Stores all n booked events.
key_insight: Two intervals [s1,e1) and [s2,e2) DON'T overlap iff e1 ≤ s2 or e2 ≤ s1 — check neighbors after binary search insertion point.

title: Design Hit Counter
hints: We only care about hits in the last 300 seconds.
Use a queue — add timestamps on hit, evict timestamps older than 300 seconds on getHits.
For high throughput, use a circular buffer of size 300 with (timestamp, count) pairs.
approach: Use a queue (deque) storing hit timestamps. On hit(), append the timestamp. On getHits(), remove all timestamps ≤ (timestamp - 300) from the front, then return the queue length.
time_complexity_explanation: Each timestamp is added once and removed once. Amortized over all operations, each is O(1).
space_complexity_explanation: The queue stores up to n hits (bounded by hits in the last 300 seconds in practice).
key_insight: A sliding window queue naturally expires old events — each element enters and exits exactly once for amortized O(1).

title: Trapping Rain Water II
hints: Water level at any cell is determined by the minimum height boundary surrounding it.
Start from the border cells (they can't hold water). Use a min-heap to process the lowest boundary cell first.
For each cell popped from the heap, try to fill its unvisited neighbors — water fills up to the current boundary height.
approach: Push all border cells into a min-heap. Process the lowest cell first. For each unvisited neighbor, the water it can trap is max(0, current_boundary - neighbor_height). Add the neighbor to the heap with height = max(current_boundary, neighbor_height). This simulates water flooding inward from the lowest boundary.
time_complexity_explanation: Each of the m×n cells is pushed/popped from the heap once. Each heap operation is O(log(m·n)).
space_complexity_explanation: The heap and visited matrix each use O(m×n) space.
key_insight: Process boundary cells from lowest to highest (min-heap). Each cell's water level is constrained by the minimum boundary path to reach it.

title: Boundary of Binary Tree
hints: The boundary has three parts: left boundary (top-down), leaves (left-right), right boundary (bottom-up).
Left boundary: go left if possible, otherwise right, until a leaf.
Right boundary: mirror of left boundary — go right if possible, otherwise left, collected in reverse.
approach: Collect three parts separately. Left boundary: walk down-left (prefer left, else right), excluding leaves. Leaves: DFS to collect all leaf nodes left to right. Right boundary: walk down-right (prefer right, else left), excluding leaves, then reverse. Combine: root + left boundary + leaves + reversed right boundary.
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited at most twice (once for boundary, once for leaf check).
space_complexity_explanation: Output list and recursion stack each use O(n) in the worst case.
key_insight: Split the boundary into three independently collected parts (left edge, leaves, right edge reversed) to avoid complex state tracking.

title: Number of Visible People in a Queue
hints: Person i can see person j (j > i) if all people between them are shorter than both i and j.
Use a monotonic decreasing stack, processing from right to left.
For each person, pop shorter people from the stack (they are visible), then peek at the next taller person (also visible if exists).
approach: Iterate from right to left with a monotonic decreasing stack. For each person, pop all shorter people from the stack — each popped person is visible. If the stack is non-empty after popping, the top person is also visible (they block further view). Push the current person.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is pushed and popped from the stack at most once — total O(n) work.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack and result array each use O(n) space.
key_insight: A monotonic decreasing stack tracks the 'visibility horizon' — popped elements are the shorter people you can see, and the remaining top is the first taller blocker.

title: Remove Duplicates from Sorted Array
hints: Use a slow pointer to track the write position for unique elements.
Since the array is sorted, duplicates are adjacent — compare each element to the previous unique one.
Only advance the write pointer when a new unique element is found.
approach: Use a write pointer starting at 1. Iterate with a read pointer. When nums[read] != nums[write-1], copy the new element to the write position and advance the write pointer. Return write as the new length.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array with one comparison per element.
space_complexity_explanation: In-place modification using only a write pointer variable.
key_insight: The sorted order guarantees duplicates are adjacent — a single write pointer is enough to compact uniques in-place.

title: Search Insert Position
hints: This is essentially bisect_left — find the leftmost position where target could be inserted.
If nums[mid] < target, search right. Otherwise, search left.
When the loop ends, lo is the insertion point.
approach: Standard lower-bound binary search. Find the leftmost index where nums[i] >= target. This is both the position of target (if present) and the correct insertion position (if absent).
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration.
space_complexity_explanation: Only pointer variables are used.
key_insight: This is the fundamental bisect_left operation — the building block for many binary search problems.

title: Linked List Cycle II
hints: Use slow and fast pointers to detect the cycle (Floyd's algorithm).
Once they meet inside the cycle, the entry point is equidistant from head and the meeting point.
Reset one pointer to head and advance both at the same speed — they'll meet at the cycle start.
approach: Phase 1: slow/fast pointers detect the cycle (fast moves 2 steps, slow 1). Phase 2: reset one pointer to head. Advance both one step at a time — they meet at the cycle entry. This works because the distance from head to cycle start equals the distance from the meeting point to cycle start (going around).
time_complexity_explanation: Both phases traverse the list at most twice in total.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointer variables are used — no hash set needed.
key_insight: Floyd's theorem: after detection, the distance from head to cycle entry equals the distance from the meeting point to cycle entry (modulo cycle length).

title: Symmetric Tree
hints: A tree is symmetric if the left subtree is a mirror of the right subtree.
Compare left.left with right.right, and left.right with right.left recursively.
Base cases: both null → symmetric; one null → not symmetric; different values → not symmetric.
approach: Write a helper isMirror(left, right). Base cases: both null returns True, one null returns False, different values returns False. Recurse: isMirror(left.left, right.right) AND isMirror(left.right, right.left).
time_complexity_explanation: Each node is visited once in the mirror comparison.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth is the tree height h.
key_insight: Mirror symmetry means left.left matches right.right and left.right matches right.left — a cross-comparison pattern.

title: Convert Sorted Array to Binary Search Tree
hints: The middle element of the sorted array becomes the root for a height-balanced BST.
Recursively build the left subtree from elements before the middle and right subtree from elements after.
This naturally produces a balanced tree because each subtree gets half the elements.
approach: Pick the middle element as root. Recursively convert the left half into the left subtree and the right half into the right subtree. The midpoint split ensures the tree is height-balanced.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element becomes exactly one node — n nodes are created.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion depth is O(log n) for a balanced tree. The output tree is O(n) but not counted as extra space.
key_insight: Choosing the middle element as root at every level guarantees the BST is height-balanced — a direct application of divide and conquer.

title: Delete Node in a BST
hints: Three cases: leaf node (just remove), one child (replace with child), two children (replace with successor).
The inorder successor is the leftmost node in the right subtree.
Replace the node's value with its successor's value, then recursively delete the successor.
approach: Recursively search for the key. When found: if leaf, return null. If one child, return that child. If two children, find the inorder successor (smallest in right subtree), copy its value, then recursively delete the successor from the right subtree.
time_complexity_explanation: We traverse down the tree at most twice (to find the node and to find/delete the successor). h is the tree height.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth is O(h).
key_insight: The two-children case is elegant: copy the inorder successor's value into the node, then delete the successor (which has at most one child).

title: Implement Queue using Stacks
hints: Use two stacks: an input stack for push, and an output stack for pop/peek.
When the output stack is empty, transfer all elements from input to output (reversing the order).
This lazy transfer gives amortized O(1) per operation.
approach: Two stacks: push to input stack. For pop/peek, if output stack is empty, pour all elements from input to output (reversing order). Then pop/peek from output. Each element is moved at most twice — amortized O(1).
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is pushed and popped from each stack at most once — amortized O(1) per operation.
space_complexity_explanation: Both stacks together hold all n elements.
key_insight: Lazy transfer: only move input→output when output is empty. This amortizes the O(n) transfer cost across n operations.

title: Add Digits
hints: Repeatedly summing the digits of any positive integer eventually reaches a single digit — this is the digital root.
There is a direct mathematical formula involving modulo 9 that avoids any looping.
The digital root of n is 0 if n == 0, else 1 + (n - 1) % 9.
approach: Use the digital root formula from number theory. For any positive integer n, the digital root is n % 9 if n % 9 != 0, or 9 if n % 9 == 0 (and n > 0). This reduces the problem to a single arithmetic operation with no loops.
time_complexity_explanation: The digital root formula computes the answer with a fixed number of arithmetic operations regardless of input size.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra data structures are used; only a constant amount of space is needed.
key_insight: The digital root of a number equals its value modulo 9 (with a special case for multiples of 9), because repeatedly summing digits is equivalent to repeatedly subtracting 9.

title: Minimum Path Sum
hints: You can only move right or down. dp[i][j] = min cost to reach cell (i, j).
dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
You can modify the grid in-place to save space.
approach: DP where dp[i][j] = grid[i][j] + min(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Initialize first row and column as cumulative sums. Fill the rest using the transition. The answer is dp[m-1][n-1].
time_complexity_explanation: We fill each cell once in the m × n grid.
space_complexity_explanation: In-place modification of the input grid — no extra space used.
key_insight: With only right/down moves, each cell's minimum path sum depends only on the cell above and to the left — classic 2D DP.

title: Triangle
hints: Process bottom-up: start from the last row and work upward.
dp[j] = triangle[i][j] + min(dp[j], dp[j+1]) where dp starts as the bottom row.
Bottom-up avoids index boundary issues since each row has one more element than the row above.
approach: Bottom-up DP. Start with the last row as the DP array. For each row above, update dp[j] = triangle[i][j] + min(dp[j], dp[j+1]). After processing row 0, dp[0] holds the minimum path sum.
time_complexity_explanation: n rows with 1 to n elements each — total work is 1+2+...+n = O(n²).
space_complexity_explanation: A single 1D DP array of size n (bottom row length).
key_insight: Bottom-up processing lets you use a 1D array — each update only needs dp[j] and dp[j+1] from the row below.

title: Single Number III
hints: XOR all numbers together — the result is xor = a ^ b where a and b are the two unique numbers.
Find any set bit in xor (e.g., the lowest set bit using xor & (-xor)) — this bit differs between a and b.
Use that distinguishing bit to partition all numbers into two groups and XOR each group separately to isolate a and b.
approach: XOR all elements to get a^b (since duplicates cancel). Find a bit that is set in a^b (use lowest set bit: diff & -diff). Partition numbers into two groups based on whether that bit is set. XOR each group separately; duplicates cancel within each group, leaving a in one and b in the other.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes over the array are made; each pass is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of integer variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: XORing all elements gives a^b; isolating any differing bit lets you split the array into two groups that each XOR to one of the two unique numbers.

title: Isomorphic Strings
hints: Two strings are isomorphic if characters in s can be mapped 1-to-1 to characters in t.
The mapping must be bijective — no two characters in s map to the same character in t, and vice versa.
Use two maps: s→t and t→s. Check consistency at each position.
approach: Maintain two hash maps: one mapping s[i]→t[i] and another mapping t[i]→s[i]. At each position, verify both maps are consistent. If any conflict is found, the strings are not isomorphic.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through both strings of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Each map stores at most 128 ASCII characters — constant space.
key_insight: Bijection requires two maps — one-way mapping misses cases like 'ab'→'aa' where two chars map to the same target.

title: Pascal's Triangle
hints: Each row starts and ends with 1.
For interior elements: row[j] = prev_row[j-1] + prev_row[j].
Build each row from the previous row, iterating from row 0 to numRows-1.
approach: Generate rows iteratively. Start with [1]. Each subsequent row has 1 at both ends, and each interior element is the sum of two elements from the row above.
time_complexity_explanation: Total elements across all rows: 1+2+...+n = n(n+1)/2 = O(n²).
space_complexity_explanation: The output stores all n² / 2 elements.
key_insight: Each element is the sum of its two parents above — build row by row from the top.

title: Find All Numbers Disappeared in an Array
hints: Each value is in the range [1, n]. Use the array itself as a hash map.
For each num, mark nums[abs(num) - 1] as negative to indicate that value exists.
After marking, indices with positive values correspond to missing numbers.
approach: Use the input array as a presence tracker. For each value, negate the element at index (value - 1). After processing, any index i with a positive value means (i + 1) was never seen.
time_complexity_explanation: Two passes through the array — one to mark, one to collect.
space_complexity_explanation: In-place negation — no extra space besides the output array.
key_insight: Negating nums[val-1] uses the array as a boolean presence set — O(1) space marking with values in [1, n].

title: Delete and Earn
hints: If you take a number x, you must delete all x-1 and x+1. This is like House Robber on a number line.
Count the total points for each number: points[x] = x * count(x).
Then solve House Robber on the points array — take or skip each number.
approach: Transform into House Robber: compute the total gain for each value (value × frequency). Create an array indexed by value. Then apply the House Robber DP: for each value, either take it (skip previous) or skip it (keep previous best).
time_complexity_explanation: Counting frequencies is O(n). The DP sweep is O(M) where M = max value.
space_complexity_explanation: The points array has M+1 entries.
key_insight: Reducing to House Robber: 'taking x deletes x±1' is equivalent to 'robbing house x means you can't rob x±1'.

title: Palindrome Number
hints: Negative numbers are never palindromes.
Can you reverse half of the number instead of the whole thing?
Compare the reversed half with the remaining half to avoid overflow.
approach: Reverse the second half of the number and compare it with the first half. Handle edge cases: negative numbers and numbers ending in 0 (except 0 itself).
time_complexity_explanation: We process half the digits of the number, so it's proportional to the number of digits.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few integer variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Reverse only the second half of the number and compare with the first half to avoid overflow issues.

title: Remove Element
hints: You need to modify the array in-place and return the new length.
Use a slow pointer to track the position for the next non-val element.
Copy non-val elements to the front of the array using the slow pointer.
approach: Use two pointers: a slow pointer tracks the write position, and a fast pointer scans through the array. Copy elements that don't equal val to the slow pointer position.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the array once with the fast pointer.
space_complexity_explanation: Modifications are done in-place with only pointer variables.
key_insight: A slow/fast pointer approach lets you overwrite unwanted elements in-place in a single pass.

title: Find the Index of the First Occurrence in a String
hints: Think about when it's impossible for needle to be found — what's the latest start index?
At each valid starting position, compare substring of length needle with needle.
You can also use built-in string methods, but understanding the manual approach is valuable.
approach: Slide a window of length len(needle) across haystack. At each position, check if the substring matches needle. Return the index of the first match or -1.
time_complexity_explanation: For each of the n-m+1 starting positions, we compare up to m characters.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space is used beyond a few variables (substring comparison is O(1) extra in some implementations).
key_insight: A simple sliding window comparison works efficiently for this problem given typical input sizes.

title: First Missing Positive
hints: The answer must be in the range [1, n+1] where n is the array length.
Try to place each number in its 'correct' position: number i at index i-1.
After placing numbers, the first index where nums[i] != i+1 gives the answer.
approach: Use cyclic sort to place each value v at index v-1. Then scan to find the first index i where nums[i] != i+1. If all are correct, return n+1.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is swapped at most once to its correct position, so total swaps are O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: The array is modified in-place with no extra data structures.
key_insight: Use the array itself as a hash map by placing each value v at index v-1 via cyclic sort.

title: Merge Sorted Array
hints: Start merging from the end of nums1 to avoid overwriting elements.
Use three pointers: one for the end of each array's valid elements and one for the write position.
After the loop, copy any remaining elements from nums2.
approach: Merge from the back of nums1. Compare elements from the end of both arrays and place the larger one at the current write position moving backwards.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element from both arrays is visited exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Merging is done in-place within nums1 using only pointer variables.
key_insight: Merge from the back of nums1 so you never overwrite unprocessed elements.

title: Restore IP Addresses
hints: An IP address has exactly 4 parts separated by dots, each between 0 and 255.
Use backtracking to try placing dots at different positions.
Watch out for leading zeros — '01' and '00' are invalid segments, but '0' is valid.
approach: Use backtracking to try all ways to split the string into 4 valid segments (0-255, no leading zeros). Collect valid combinations.
time_complexity_explanation: The input is at most 12 characters and there are at most 3^4 = 81 combinations to try, so it's bounded by a constant.
space_complexity_explanation: The number of valid IP addresses is bounded by a constant, and recursion depth is at most 4.
key_insight: Backtrack through all ways to split the string into 4 segments, validating each segment is 0-255 with no leading zeros.

title: Best Time to Buy and Sell Stock III
hints: You can make at most 2 transactions. Think about tracking state for each transaction.
Track the minimum cost to buy for each transaction (accounting for previous profit).
Use four variables: buy1, sell1, buy2, sell2 to track the best state at each step.
approach: Use a state machine with four variables representing the best profit after first buy, first sell, second buy, and second sell. Update all four in a single pass.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the prices array once.
space_complexity_explanation: Only four variables are used regardless of input size.
key_insight: Track the effective cost of the second buy as price minus first profit, allowing a single-pass solution with four state variables.

title: Candy
hints: Each child must get at least one candy.
Try two passes: left-to-right for increasing sequences, right-to-left for decreasing.
Take the maximum of both passes for each position to satisfy both neighbors.
approach: Two-pass greedy: first pass left-to-right ensures right neighbor with higher rating gets more candy. Second pass right-to-left ensures left neighbor with higher rating gets more. Take the max at each position.
time_complexity_explanation: Two linear passes through the ratings array.
space_complexity_explanation: An array of size n is used to store the candy count for each child.
key_insight: Two greedy passes (left-to-right and right-to-left) independently satisfy each direction's constraints; taking the max at each index satisfies both.

title: Find Peak Element
hints: A peak element is greater than its neighbors. The edges are treated as negative infinity.
If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], a peak must exist to the right.
Use binary search — always move toward the higher neighbor.
approach: Binary search: compare mid with mid+1. If nums[mid] < nums[mid+1], the peak is to the right; otherwise it's to the left (or at mid). Converge to a peak in O(log n).
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the search space each iteration.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a few pointer variables are used.
key_insight: Binary search works because moving toward the higher neighbor guarantees convergence to a peak.

title: Article Views I
hints: An author views their own article when author_id equals viewer_id.
Use DISTINCT to avoid duplicate author IDs in the result.
Sort the result by id in ascending order.
approach: SELECT DISTINCT author_id AS id FROM Views WHERE author_id = viewer_id ORDER BY id ASC;
solution_python: SELECT DISTINCT author_id AS id FROM Views WHERE author_id = viewer_id ORDER BY id ASC;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans through all rows in the Views table once.
space_complexity_explanation: Space for storing the distinct author IDs in the result set.
key_insight: Filter rows where author_id equals viewer_id and use DISTINCT to remove duplicates.

title: First Bad Version
hints: The versions form a sorted sequence: all good versions come before all bad versions.
Binary search works here — if mid is bad, the first bad version is at mid or earlier; if good, it must be after mid.
Track the leftmost bad version found and narrow the search window accordingly.
approach: Apply binary search over the version range [1, n]. At each midpoint, call isBadVersion(mid). If it returns true, the first bad version is at mid or to the left, so move right to mid. If false, the first bad version is to the right, so move left to mid + 1. Continue until left == right, which is the first bad version.
time_complexity_explanation: Binary search halves the search space at each step, requiring at most log2(n) calls to isBadVersion.
space_complexity_explanation: Only two pointer variables are maintained regardless of n.
key_insight: Because all good versions precede all bad versions, the problem is equivalent to finding the left boundary in a binary search, minimizing the number of expensive API calls.

title: Recyclable and Low Fat Products
hints: You need products that are both low fat AND recyclable.
Use a WHERE clause with AND to combine both conditions.
Low fat is indicated by low_fats = 'Y' and recyclable by recyclable = 'Y'.
approach: SELECT product_id FROM Products WHERE low_fats = 'Y' AND recyclable = 'Y';
solution_python: SELECT product_id FROM Products WHERE low_fats = 'Y' AND recyclable = 'Y';
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The query scans all rows in the Products table.
space_complexity_explanation: Space for storing matching product IDs in the result set.
key_insight: Simple WHERE clause filtering on two column conditions joined by AND.

title: Frequency of the Most Frequent Element
hints: Sort the array first — you only need to increment elements to match the largest in a window.
Use a sliding window and track the total cost to make all elements in the window equal to the rightmost.
Shrink the window from the left when the cost exceeds k.
approach: Sort the array. Use a sliding window where all elements are incremented to match the right boundary. Track the total increments needed; shrink the window when it exceeds k.
time_complexity_explanation: Dominated by sorting. The sliding window pass is O(n).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is in-place and only a few variables are used for the sliding window.
key_insight: After sorting, the cost to make a window of elements equal to the maximum is efficiently tracked with a sliding window.

title: Recover Binary Search Tree
hints: An in-order traversal of a valid BST produces a sorted sequence.
Two swapped nodes create one or two inversions in the in-order sequence.
Track the first and second misplaced nodes during in-order traversal, then swap their values.
approach: Perform in-order traversal tracking the previous node. The first inversion gives the first swapped node; the second inversion (or same inversion) gives the second. Swap their values.
time_complexity_explanation: In-order traversal visits every node exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth is proportional to the tree height h.
key_insight: In-order traversal of a BST is sorted; two swapped nodes create inversions that can be detected in a single pass.

title: 4Sum
hints: Sort the array first, then reduce to a two-sum problem with two outer loops.
Use two nested loops for the first two numbers, then a two-pointer approach for the remaining two.
Skip duplicates at each level to avoid duplicate quadruplets.
approach: Sort the array. Use two nested loops for the first two elements, then use two pointers for the remaining pair. Skip duplicates at every level.
time_complexity_explanation: Two nested loops O(n^2) with an inner two-pointer scan O(n) gives O(n^3).
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is in-place and only pointer variables are used (excluding output).
key_insight: Reduce 4Sum to 2Sum by fixing two elements with nested loops and using two pointers for the remaining pair.

title: Longest Valid Parentheses
hints: A stack can help match parentheses and track the start of valid substrings.
Push indices onto the stack. When you find a match, the length is current index minus the new stack top.
Initialize the stack with -1 as a base for length calculation.
approach: Use a stack initialized with -1. Push indices of '(' and on ')' pop the stack. If stack is empty push current index as new base, otherwise compute length as i - stack top.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the string with O(1) operations per character.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack can hold up to n indices in the worst case.
key_insight: Use a stack with a sentinel base index (-1) to efficiently track the start boundary of valid parentheses substrings.

title: Subarrays with K Different Integers
hints: Counting subarrays with exactly K distinct values is hard directly.
Use the identity: exactly(K) = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1).
Implement an atMost(K) helper using a sliding window with a frequency map.
approach: Use the formula: exactly(K) = atMost(K) - atMost(K-1). Implement atMost using a sliding window that shrinks when distinct count exceeds the limit.
time_complexity_explanation: Each call to atMost is O(n) with the sliding window, and we call it twice.
space_complexity_explanation: The frequency map holds at most k+1 entries.
key_insight: Convert the exact-K problem into two at-most-K sliding window problems using the subtraction trick.

title: Add Two Integers
hints: This problem is straightforward — just add the two numbers.
Use the addition operator.
Return num1 + num2.
approach: Simply return the sum of the two input integers.
time_complexity_explanation: A single addition operation is performed.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space is used.
key_insight: This is a trivial problem testing basic arithmetic — just return the sum.

title: H-Index
hints: The h-index is the largest h such that h papers have at least h citations each.
Sort citations in descending order and find where citations[i] >= i+1 stops being true.
Alternatively, use a counting sort approach for O(n) time.
approach: Sort citations in descending order. Iterate and find the largest h where citations[i] >= i+1. The answer is the last valid i+1.
time_complexity_explanation: Dominated by the sorting step.
space_complexity_explanation: Sorting is done in-place with only a few extra variables.
key_insight: After sorting in descending order, the h-index is the largest index i+1 where citations[i] >= i+1.

title: Ransom Note
hints: Count the frequency of each character in the magazine.
Check if the magazine has enough of each character needed by the ransom note.
Use a hash map or array of size 26 for character counting.
approach: Count character frequencies in the magazine, then verify the ransom note can be constructed by checking each character has sufficient count.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through both the magazine (m) and ransom note (n) once each.
space_complexity_explanation: The character frequency map uses at most 26 entries (constant space for lowercase English letters).
key_insight: Count character frequencies in the magazine and verify the ransom note doesn't need more of any character than available.

title: Rising Temperature
hints: You need to compare each day's temperature with the previous day's.
Join the Weather table with itself where the date difference is exactly 1 day.
Use DATEDIFF to find rows where today's temperature is higher than yesterday's.
approach: SELECT w1.id FROM Weather w1 JOIN Weather w2 ON DATEDIFF(w1.recordDate, w2.recordDate) = 1 WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature;
solution_python: SELECT w1.id FROM Weather w1 JOIN Weather w2 ON DATEDIFF(w1.recordDate, w2.recordDate) = 1 WHERE w1.temperature > w2.temperature;
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The self-join compares each row with every other row, though indexing can optimize this.
space_complexity_explanation: Space for storing the joined result set.
key_insight: Self-join the Weather table on consecutive dates using DATEDIFF and filter where temperature increased.

title: Big Countries
hints: Think about what conditions define a 'big' country.
You need countries with area >= 3000000 OR population >= 25000000.
Use a simple SELECT with a WHERE clause using OR.
approach: Filter rows using WHERE with OR condition on area and population thresholds.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: Scans each row once to check the conditions.
space_complexity_explanation: No additional space beyond the result set.
key_insight: Use OR to combine two independent filtering conditions in a single WHERE clause.

title: Fizz Buzz
hints: Iterate from 1 to n and check divisibility conditions.
Check divisibility by 15 first (both 3 and 5), then by 3, then by 5.
Use the modulo operator to check divisibility.
approach: Iterate 1 to n. For each number check divisibility by 15, 3, and 5 in order, appending the appropriate string.
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through each number from 1 to n exactly once.
space_complexity_explanation: The result array stores n strings.
key_insight: Check divisibility by 15 before checking 3 or 5 individually to handle the combined case.

title: Count Good Numbers
hints: Even indices (0-indexed) can have 5 choices (0,2,4,6,8) and odd indices can have 4 choices (2,3,5,7).
Count how many even and odd positions there are for a string of length n.
Use modular exponentiation to efficiently compute large powers modulo 10^9+7.
approach: For a string of length n, even positions have 5 choices and odd positions have 4 choices. Compute 5^(ceil(n/2)) * 4^(floor(n/2)) mod 10^9+7 using fast exponentiation.
time_complexity_explanation: Modular exponentiation uses binary exponentiation which runs in O(log n).
space_complexity_explanation: Only a constant number of variables are used.
key_insight: The total count is 5^(even positions) * 4^(odd positions), computed efficiently with modular exponentiation.

title: Combine Two Tables
hints: You need information from both Person and Address tables.
Not every person has an address, so you need a LEFT JOIN to keep all persons.
Join on the personId column that exists in both tables.
approach: Use a LEFT JOIN from Person to Address on personId to include all persons even if they have no address.
solution_js: -- SQL Problem: No JavaScript solution applicable
time_complexity_explanation: The join processes each row from both tables.
space_complexity_explanation: No additional space beyond the result set.
key_insight: LEFT JOIN ensures every person appears in the result even without a matching address.

title: Permutations II
hints: Sort the array first so duplicates are adjacent.
Use a visited array and skip a number if it's the same as the previous one and the previous one wasn't used in this branch.
Backtrack by unmarking visited and removing from the current path.
approach: Sort the array, then use backtracking with a visited array. Skip duplicates by checking if the current element equals the previous and the previous wasn't visited.
time_complexity_explanation: In the worst case we generate n! permutations, each taking O(n) to copy.
space_complexity_explanation: Recursion stack depth is O(n), plus the used array of size n.
key_insight: Sort first, then skip duplicate elements at the same recursion level to avoid generating duplicate permutations.

title: Longest Substring with At Most K Distinct Characters
hints: Use a sliding window with two pointers and a hash map to track character counts.
Expand the right pointer and add characters. When distinct characters exceed k, shrink from the left.
Track the maximum window size throughout the process.
approach: Use a sliding window with a hash map counting character frequencies. Expand right, and when distinct count exceeds k, shrink from left until valid again.
time_complexity_explanation: Each character is added and removed from the window at most once.
space_complexity_explanation: The hash map stores at most k+1 distinct characters.
key_insight: A sliding window with a hash map efficiently tracks the number of distinct characters in the current substring.

title: Online Stock Span
hints: Think about how many consecutive previous days had a price <= today's price.
Use a stack that stores (price, span) pairs.
When a new price comes in, pop all stack entries with price <= current price and accumulate their spans.
approach: Maintain a monotonic decreasing stack of (price, span) pairs. For each new price, pop entries with smaller or equal prices, summing their spans into the current span.
time_complexity_explanation: Each element is pushed and popped from the stack at most once across all calls.
space_complexity_explanation: The stack can hold up to n elements in the worst case.
key_insight: A monotonic decreasing stack lets you accumulate spans by absorbing smaller elements as they are popped.

title: Check if Array Is Sorted and Rotated
hints: In a sorted and rotated array, there is at most one point where the order decreases.
Count the number of places where nums[i] > nums[i+1] (including wrap-around).
If the count is 0 or 1, the array is sorted and rotated.
approach: Count the number of inversions (nums[i] > nums[(i+1) % n]). A valid sorted-and-rotated array has at most one inversion.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array of length n.
space_complexity_explanation: Only a counter variable is used.
key_insight: A sorted-and-rotated array has at most one 'break point' where the value decreases, including the wrap-around from last to first element.

title: Find All Duplicates in an Array
hints: Elements are in range [1, n] where n is the array length. Use the array indices as a hash.
For each number, negate the value at the index corresponding to that number.
If the value at that index is already negative, the number is a duplicate.
approach: Use the array itself as a hash map. For each value, mark the element at that index as negative. If it's already negative, the value is a duplicate.
time_complexity_explanation: Single pass through the array.
space_complexity_explanation: No extra space used apart from the result array; modifications are in-place.
key_insight: Since values are in [1, n], use the sign of elements at corresponding indices to detect duplicates without extra space.

title: Add Strings
hints: Simulate addition digit by digit from right to left, just like manual addition.
Use two pointers starting at the end of each string and track the carry.
Don't forget to handle the remaining carry after processing all digits.
approach: Simulate grade-school addition from right to left using two pointers and a carry variable. Build the result string in reverse.
solution_python:
class Solution:
    def addStrings(self, num1: str, num2: str) -> str:
        i, j = len(num1) - 1, len(num2) - 1
        carry = 0
        result = []
        while i >= 0 or j >= 0 or carry:
            x = int(num1[i]) if i >= 0 else 0
            y = int(num2[j]) if j >= 0 else 0
            total = x + y + carry
            result.append(str(total % 10))
            carry = total // 10
            i -= 1
            j -= 1
        return ''.join(reversed(result))
time_complexity_explanation: We iterate through the longer of the two strings.
space_complexity_explanation: The result string has length proportional to the longer input.
key_insight: Process digits from right to left with a carry, just like manual addition on paper.

title: Maximal Rectangle
